quote.text: The brain is wider than the sky, for, put them side by side, the one the other will contain with ease, and you beside.
quote.author: Emily Dickinson

eppp.question: A psychologist receives a subpoena from an attorney requesting the complete clinical record of a current client. The client has not authorized release. What is the psychologist's MOST appropriate first action?
options: Comply with the subpoena immediately, since it is a legal document
Ignore the subpoena because the client has not consented
Contact the client and consult with an attorney to clarify obligations and options
Release only the intake summary to demonstrate good-faith cooperation
eppp.eli5: A subpoena is like a letter asking for the toy box, but it is not the same as a judge saying you MUST hand it over. Before sharing private things, the psychologist talks to the client and asks a lawyer what to do. That keeps the client's secrets safe and follows the rules.
whyOthersWrong: A subpoena alone is not a court order, so handing everything over right away can break confidentiality.
Ignoring it is risky and can get the psychologist in legal trouble; you must respond, just carefully.
Sharing even part of the record without permission or legal guidance still leaks private information.

study.title: Beyond the Gym: Why Your Brain Craves Creatine
study.eli5: Scientists looked at creatine, the stuff people take to get stronger at the gym. They wanted to see what it does inside the brain. They found it helps brain cells make energy faster, like recharging tiny batteries. This might help people think more clearly, especially as they get older.
study.psydAnalysis: This summary (working from the Neuroscience News write-up rather than the original paper's abstract, which I could not cleanly match) frames creatine as a brain-energy helper, not just a muscle supplement. The proposed mechanism is faster regeneration of ATP, the molecule cells use as fuel, which matters because the brain is one of the body's hungriest organs. For clinical psychology, this is relevant to neurocognitive aging, fatigue-related cognitive complaints, and lifestyle factors clients often ask about. A future clinician will not prescribe supplements, but understanding the literature helps with informed conversations and appropriate referrals to physicians or neurologists. It also reinforces the biopsychosocial frame: cognition rests on metabolic substrates, and energy supply may interact with mood, attention, and processing speed. Treat this as an interesting hypothesis-generating signal, not a settled clinical recommendation.
strengths: Targets a clear, testable mechanism (ATP regeneration) rather than a vague wellness claim.
Highlights relevance to aging brains, a high-need clinical population.
Connects a widely available, well-studied compound to cognitive outcomes, easing translation.
Encourages integration of metabolic and neurological thinking into mental health care.
weaknesses: The journalist summary does not report sample size, design, or effect sizes, so rigor is unclear.
No information on whether findings come from animal models, humans, or both.
Dosage, duration, and safety details are not specified in the summary.
Generalizability across age, sex, diet, and clinical populations is unaddressed.
Publication and replication status of the underlying paper cannot be verified from this source alone.
study.validationNote: Source article from Neuroscience News — primary paper not independently matched on PubMed
